does: This paper introduces TPOUR, an unsupervised dense retriever that learns to incorporate temporal relevance without labeled data. The core contribution is Temporal Retrieval Preference Optimization (TRPO), a novel training method that reframes preference learning to favor documents temporally aligned with query context. TPOUR extends this by learning a continuous time embedding that generalizes to unseen time periods through interpolation, enabling the retriever to handle temporal queries across different decades without explicit timestamp supervision. Experiments on temporal information retrieval benchmarks demonstrate that TPOUR outperforms both unsupervised baselines that ignore temporal signals and supervised methods requiring labeled temporal annotations, addressing a critical limitation of existing contrastive learning approaches that conflate semantic and temporal relevance.
connects: This work builds on the recent success of unsupervised dense retrievers trained via contrastive learning, which have achieved strong performance on semantic retrieval but remain limited to capturing shallow textual similarity. The paper extends prior work on preference optimization and ranking-based learning by reconceptualizing it in the temporal domain, connecting to broader research on time-aware information retrieval that previously relied on supervised training with explicit timestamps. It responds to known gaps in dense retrieval where semantically relevant documents from different time periods can be incorrectly ranked, a problem increasingly relevant as retrieval systems operate over temporally distributed collections. The approach also relates to recent progress in embedding space methods for handling temporal dynamics, though applied here to the unsupervised retrieval setting.
enables: This work opens several future directions for handling temporal aspects in dense retrieval without supervision. The continuous time embedding approach could generalize to other structured metadata dimensions beyond timestamps, suggesting a path toward unsupervised retrievers that respect multiple document attributes. The TRPO framework might extend to other preference signals beyond temporal alignment—such as document recency, popularity decay, or domain-specific relevance shifts—enabling more nuanced ranking objectives. Results suggest future work on jointly learning semantic and temporal representations, which could improve retrieval for time-sensitive applications like news search, historical document retrieval, and question answering over evolving knowledge. The method could also inform research on temporal generalization in language models and retrieval systems that must adapt to distribution shifts across time periods.
